critic_raw:
The phase retrieval paper proposes an unrolled network that learns a reference signal added to the unknown input before Fourier magnitude measurement. The central claims — generalization to unseen distributions and robustness to spatial augmentations — both rest on assumptions that the small-data training regime makes difficult to assess.

The 'learned reference' is claimed to generalize well to unseen data distributions. An unrolled network learns algorithm parameters tuned to the training data distribution; generalization here means the learned reference and iteration parameters remain useful when the statistical properties of the unknown signal change. But this is precisely where unrolled networks are known to fail: the unrolling assumes a fixed number of iterations sufficient for convergence, and when applied to signals from a different distribution, the iterates may not converge in the same number of steps, producing degraded outputs without visible failure. The paper does not report error as a function of distribution shift magnitude (e.g., held-out natural images when trained on faces).

The robustness claim under shifting and rotation is specific and testable, but the mechanism is underspecified. Physical phase retrieval systems produce Fourier magnitudes where spatial shifts of the input correspond to phase changes that are precisely canceled by the magnitude operation — so shift invariance in Fourier magnitude measurements is a mathematical property, not an empirical achievement. If shift invariance holds automatically in Fourier domain phase retrieval, claiming robustness to shifting is not a meaningful contribution. The paper should separate mathematical invariance (baked into the measurement model) from learned robustness (genuine empirical contribution).

Training on 'a small amount of data' is both a feature claim (low data requirement) and an unacknowledged confound. The quality of the learned reference depends on how representative the small training set is of the in-distribution signal class. If the training set is small but highly structured (e.g., centered, cropped face images), the reference may specialize to that structure rather than generalizing. The paper does not report how performance degrades as training set size decreases — which would characterize the actual data efficiency and distinguish it from overfitting to a convenient dataset.

The validation uses 'open-access datasets' but does not describe what makes these datasets appropriate test cases for the distributional generalization claim. If the open-access test sets are drawn from the same underlying distribution as the training data (common in face datasets where all images come from the same controlled capture process), the generalization claim is not tested.
all_issues_raised: Unrolled networks are known to fail under distribution shift because the fixed iteration count may be insufficient for convergence on out-of-distribution inputs; the paper does not report reconstruction error as a function of distribution shift magnitude.

The claimed robustness to spatial shifting is partially a mathematical property of Fourier magnitude measurements (shift equals phase change, which magnitudes ignore), not purely an empirical achievement — the paper does not separate mathematical invariance from learned robustness.

Training on a small dataset may cause the learned reference to specialize to the structural properties of that dataset (e.g., centering and framing artifacts of a face corpus) rather than learning a general-purpose reference; performance as a function of training set size is not reported.

The open-access validation datasets may share distributional properties with the training data, meaning the generalization test is not a genuine out-of-distribution evaluation — the specific datasets are not described in sufficient detail to assess this.
all_issues_adjudicated: Unrolled networks are known to fail under distribution shift because the fixed iteration count may be insufficient for convergence on out-of-distribution inputs; the paper does not report reconstruction error as a function of distribution shift magnitude.

The claimed robustness to spatial shifting is partially a mathematical property of Fourier magnitude measurements (shift equals phase change, which magnitudes ignore), not purely an empirical achievement — the paper does not separate mathematical invariance from learned robustness.

Training on a small dataset may cause the learned reference to specialize to the structural properties of that dataset (e.g., centering and framing artifacts of a face corpus) rather than learning a general-purpose reference; performance as a function of training set size is not reported.

The open-access validation datasets may share distributional properties with the training data, meaning the generalization test is not a genuine out-of-distribution evaluation — the specific datasets are not described in sufficient detail to assess this.
